title: S4/G4: Sufficiency via isotropy separation — the core hard lemma (incomplete O)

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**The main mathematical content (hardest gap).** Converse direction: if $\Phi^H X$ is $f_{\aO}(n,H)$-connective for all $\aO$-relevant $H\le G$, then $X\in\tau^{\aO}_{\ge n}$.

**To prove (suggested route).**
1. Conservativity / connectivity-detection: the family $\{\Phi^H\}_{H\le G}$ detects connectivity on bounded-below $G$-spectra.
2. Induct on $|G|$. Use the isotropy-separation cofiber sequence $(E\mathcal F)_+\wedge X\to X\to \widetilde{E\mathcal F}\wedge X$ for a family $\mathcal F$ compatible with $\aO$: $\widetilde{E\mathcal F}\wedge X$ encodes $\Phi^G X$, and $(E\mathcal F)_+\wedge X$ is governed by proper-subgroup data handled by induction.
3. Show both pieces lie in $\tau^{\aO}_{\ge n}$ under the hypotheses, and that the extension stays in $\tau^{\aO}_{\ge n}$ **using only $\aO$-admissible norms/transfers** — this is exactly where incompleteness of $\aO$ obstructs the classical HHR re-assembly and the induction must be re-engineered.

This is research-level; the classical sufficiency proofs use the full transfer/norm structure unavailable for incomplete $\aO$. Reference: HHR Reduction/Slice theorems; Ullman, *On the slice spectral sequence*.
